evals.name: hn-frontpage-single-screen
evals.prompt: Can you review the UX of the Hacker News front page (https://news.ycombinator.com/)? I want to know what usability problems it has and how to fix them.
evals.expected_output: A markdown UX audit file. Findings tied to specific named Laws of UX with visible evidence from the actual HN page (e.g. tiny tap targets / thin links -> Fitts's Law, dense undifferentiated rows -> Chunking/Proximity). Each finding has severity, effort, and a concrete fix. Ends with an impact-ranked action plan.
expectations: A markdown report file was created in the run's outputs directory.
Findings cite at least 3 distinct, correctly-named Laws of UX.
Every finding carries a severity label (Critical / Major / Minor).
Every finding carries an implementation-effort label (S / M / L).
The report ends with a prioritized action plan or checklist ordered by impact vs. effort.
Each finding includes a concrete, specific fix that names what to change, not a restatement of the problem.
Findings are grounded in specific observed elements of the actual page, not generic UX advice.
At least one finding addresses HN's actual small or densely packed interactive targets and ties it to Fitts's Law and/or Proximity/Chunking.

evals.name: saucedemo-checkout-flow
evals.prompt: Audit the UX of the shopping + checkout flow on https://www.saucedemo.com/ (log in with the standard_user / secret_sauce credentials shown on the login page, add an item to the cart, and go through checkout). Where does the flow fall down and what should we fix first?
evals.expected_output: A markdown UX audit covering multiple screens (login, inventory, cart, checkout steps, confirmation). Per-screen findings tied to named laws with evidence, plus cross-flow observations. Prioritized action plan.
expectations: A markdown report file was created in the run's outputs directory.
Findings cite at least 3 distinct, correctly-named Laws of UX.
Every finding carries a severity label (Critical / Major / Minor).
Every finding carries an implementation-effort label (S / M / L).
The report ends with a prioritized action plan or checklist ordered by impact vs. effort.
Each finding includes a concrete, specific fix that names what to change, not a restatement of the problem.
Findings are grounded in specific observed elements of the actual flow, not generic UX advice.
The audit covers multiple distinct screens of the flow.
The report includes at least one cross-flow observation spanning screens (e.g. checkout progress tied to Goal-Gradient/Zeigarnik, or the confirmation/end moment tied to Peak-End Rule).

evals.name: govuk-mobile-restraint
evals.prompt: Give the GOV.UK homepage (https://www.gov.uk/) a mobile UX audit against UX best practices, as if viewed on a phone. It's a pretty well-regarded site so I mostly want to know if there's anything genuinely worth fixing.
evals.expected_output: A markdown UX audit captured at a mobile viewport. Because GOV.UK is a strong UI, a good result shows RESTRAINT: few findings, honest severities, an explicit 'what's working' acknowledgement, and no invented/padded problems. Any findings cite named laws with real evidence.
expectations: A markdown report file was created in the run's outputs directory.
The audit was performed at a mobile/phone viewport.
Findings cite correctly-named Laws of UX with real evidence.
Every finding carries a severity label and an effort label.
The report ends with a prioritized action plan or checklist.
Each finding includes a concrete, specific fix, not a restatement of the problem.
The report shows restraint appropriate to a strong UI: it acknowledges what works and does not fabricate Critical-severity problems without visible evidence.
The number of findings is modest and proportionate; the report is not padded with invented or trivial issues.
